1: hôdînû lĕkhā ʾĕlōhîm hôdînû — we give thanks to you, O God; we give thanks. The doubled hiphil of yādāh (to confess, acknowledge, thank) creates liturgical emphasis — thanks given and given again. wĕqārôb shĕmekā — your name is near. Qārôb (near, close) as a theological declaration: the divine name is not distant or inaccessible but close enough to invoke. The nearness of the name is the reason for thanks, not the occasion for it.
2: kî ʾeqqaḥ môʿēd — when I choose the appointed time. The voice shifts from community (v1) to God himself (vv2-5) — a divine speech embedded in the psalm. Môʿēd (appointed time, set season — the same word for the sacred calendar of Lev 23) — God controls when judgment falls. ʾănî mêshārîm ʾeshpōṭ — I will judge with perfect equity. Mêshārîm (uprightnesses, level places — plural abstract noun) — justice rendered with complete, undeflecting fairness across every dimension.
3: nĕmōgîm ʾereṣ wĕkhol-yōshĕbêhā — when the earth totters and all its people with it. Māmag (to melt, dissolve, totter) — the earth losing its structural integrity. ʾānōkî tikkantî ʿammûdêhā selāh — it is I who hold its pillars firm. The hiphil of kûn (to establish, fix, make firm) — God maintaining the structural supports of creation against dissolution. ʿAmmûdêhā (its pillars) — the cosmological framework of the earth (cf. 1 Samuel 2:8; Psalms 104:5). The selah pause marks the weight of the claim: universal chaos does not shake the one who sustains all things.
4: ʾāmartî lahalĕlîm ʾal-tāhōlû — I say to the arrogant: Stop your boasting. Hōlēl (arrogant, boastful — from hālal, the root of hallĕlûyāh — praise — turned into its degenerate opposite: vain boasting). wĕlārĕshāʿîm ʾal-tārîmû qāren — and to the wicked: do not raise your horn. The horn (qeren) is the animal's weapon and status symbol — raised high means asserting dominance. The divine speech commands the gesture of self-elevation to stop.
5: ʾal-tārîmû lammārôm qarnĕkhem — do not raise your horn on high. The repetition of the horn-command (v4b) intensifies with lammārôm (on high, toward the heights) — not just raised but raised toward heaven — the posture of those who challenge God himself. tĕdabbĕrû bĕṣawwāʾr ʿātāq — do not speak with an arrogant neck. Ṣawwāʾr ʿātāq (arrogant/stiff neck) — ʿātāq (bold, insolent, stiff — used of the neck that refuses to bow) in this construction. Speaking with a stiff neck is the posture of those who will not bow before the divine judge of v2.
6: kî lōʾ mimmôṣāʾ ûmimmaʿărāb wĕlōʾ mimmidbar — for advancement comes neither from east nor west, nor from the wilderness of the south. The directional inventory (east, west, wilderness/south) is a merism for everywhere — the negative is absolute: no human quarter, no political alliance, no geographic advantage produces the exaltation the wicked seek by raising their horns. The verse omits the north — traditionally the direction of the divine throne (Isaiah 14:13) — because v7 supplies the answer: elevation comes from God alone.
7: kî-ʾĕlōhîm shōpēṭ — but God is the judge. The participial form shōpēṭ (the one who judges — ongoing, characteristic action, not a one-time event) — judging is what God does by nature. zeh yashpîl wĕzeh yārîm — he brings one person down and raises another up. The hiphil of shāpal (to bring low) and rûm (to raise) — both vertical movements are divine prerogative. The wicked's raised horn (vv4-5) and the righteous person's lifted horn (v10) are explained by this sovereign divine action.
8: kî khôs bĕyad-YHWH wĕyayin ḥāmar — for in the hand of the LORD there is a cup of foaming wine. Kôs (cup) as the vessel of divine judgment — a major prophetic motif: Isaiah 51:17,22; Jeremiah 25:15-17; Ezekiel 23:31-33; Habakkuk 2:16; Revelation 14:10; Psalms 16:19. Yāyin ḥāmar (wine that ferments, foams) — spiced wine, full strength. wayyaṣṣēq mizzeh wĕkol-rĕshāʿê ʾāreṣ yimṣûʾ yishĕtû ʾet-shĕmārêhā — he pours it out, and all the wicked of the earth shall drain it to the very dregs. Shĕmārîm (dregs, sediment at the bottom) — the bitterest and most potent part. The wicked must drink every last drop.
9: waʾănî ʾaggîd lĕʿôlām — but as for me, I will declare this forever. The voice returns to the psalmist in a vow of perpetual proclamation. The contrast with the wicked's fate (v8) is stark: they drain the cup of wrath; the Psalmist sings forever. ʾăzammĕrāh lêʾlōhê yaʿăqōb — I will sing praises to the God of Jacob. ʾĕlōhê yaʿăqōb (God of Jacob) — not a generic deity but the God of covenant history, the one who chose the unlikely son over the elder (Genesis 25:23), whose faithfulness to Jacob is the basis for faithfulness now.
10: wĕkhol-qarnê rĕshāʿîm ʾăgaddēaʿ — all the horns of the wicked I will cut down. Gādaʿ (to cut off, hew down — used of felling trees and vines) — the horn imagery of vv4-5 reaches its resolution. Who speaks? The divine voice of vv2-5 resuming, or the psalmist confidently declaring what God will do? The ambiguity is likely intentional — the psalmist so identifies with the divine purpose that the distinction dissolves. tĕrômnāh qarnôt ṣaddîq — the horns of the righteous shall be lifted high. The contrast is complete: wicked horns cut down, righteous horns raised — the divine inversion of v7 enacted in full.
